content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 10, 2016

Ms. Susan Shelley
P.O. Box 6823
Woodland Hills, CA 91365

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-16-035

Dear Ms. Shelley:

This letter responds to your request for advice from the Fair Political Practices Commission
("FPPC") regarding campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTIONS

1. Where you have been named individually in an enforcement action as well as on behalf of
two committees for filing campaign statements late, can you legally raise money in a "go-fund-me"
style account from friends and supporters to pay FPPC fines without involving the campaign
committee?

2. If you raise money into the "Susan Shelley for Assembly 2014" committee, ID #1355796
(the "2014 committee"), which remains active because it has debt, can those funds be used to pay
FPPC fines, and if so, how is that to be accounted for? Does it affect the debt total or is it a separate
category?

3. Can you open a legal defense committee to raise the funds? If you do, what are the
contribution limits and can donors who "maxed out" to your campaign still contribute to it?

CONCLUSIONS

1. No, under the Act you may not raise campaign funds to pay for fines in a "go-fund-me"
style account.

2. Given that paying the late filing fines is a permissible use of campaign funds under
Sections 89513(c) and 89514, and that the fines would constitute additional debt of the committee,
you may raise funds into the 2014 committee under the applicable contribution limit to pay those
fines.

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-035
Page No. 2

3. Yes. Under the Act you are also permitted to open a legal defense fund to raise funds to pay the fine for late filing of campaign statements. As discussed below, contribution limits do not apply to amounts raised into a legal defense fund, and donors who gave the maximum amount to your campaign committee may contribute to the legal defense fund. (Section 85304 and Regulation 18530.4.)

FACTS²

You were a candidate for Assembly District 45 in the 2013 special primary and general elections, and ran again for that seat in the June 3, 2014 primary election. You did not win either election. You have been informed that the FPPC is pursuing an enforcement action against you for campaign finance reporting errors and omissions from your 2013 special election campaign for Assembly District 45.

Because of the circumstances of the special election – the incumbent Assembly member had won a Los Angeles City Council seat but did not resign from the Assembly for nearly four months – your campaign first had to open a 2014 committee in order to legally raise money, and then when the special election was officially called, the campaign had to open a 2013 committee in order to legally spend money. As a result of this unusual situation, loans that you made to the campaign for the special election were made to the 2014 committee, which still has $27,000 in debt. The "Susan Shelley for Assembly 2013" committee, ID #1358945 (the "2013 committee") has since been terminated.

This was a relatively small campaign that did not have any paid staff and you acted as your own treasurer. Because it was a special election, the filing deadlines were in a compressed election calendar. As one example, the campaign was required to file two pre-election reports for two committees for two elections within a time-span of 90 days. You have paid $1,590 in fines to the Secretary of State for late filing of several campaign statements.

In order to pay potential fines imposed by the Commission, you seek advice about raising funds to pay these fines. Facing potential FPPC fines, you do not want to compound the legal difficulty by incorrectly filing reports on the fundraising that you want to undertake to pay the fines.

APPLICABLE LAW AND ANALYSIS

Restrictions on Post-Election Fundraising. The Act imposes contribution limits on candidates running for state senate of $4,200 per election for 2015-16 ($4,100 for 2013-14). After the election is over, a candidate may not continue to raise funds for that election, unless the candidate has net debt outstanding from the election and needs to raise funds within the contribution limits of the election to pay the debt from that election. (Section 85316 and Regulation 18531.61.)

² The FPPC does not act as a finder of fact when providing advice and relies on the facts provided by the requester. Any errors or misinformation in the facts provided could alter the conclusions in this advice letter.

File No. A-16-035
Page No. 3

Section 85316 provides:

"(a) Except as provided in subdivision (b) [referring to state officeholder accounts], a contribution for an election may be accepted by a candidate for elective state office after the date of the election only to the extent that the contribution does not exceed net debts outstanding from the election, and the contribution does not otherwise exceed the applicable contribution limit for that election."

Regulation 18531.61 defines "net debts outstanding" and provides that a candidate for state elective office may only accept a contribution after the election under Section 85316(a) for payment of outstanding debt. A late filing fine incurred by a committee after the election would constitute additional net debt of the committee. (See e.g., Regulation 18404.1(g) and Boling Advice Letter, No. A-05-110.)

Use of campaign funds for a fine, penalty, judgment or settlement. The Act's provisions governing the use of campaign funds are found in Sections 89510-89518. Under Section 89510, campaign funds are held in trust to be used for the campaign. Section 89512 provides that candidates and elected officials are permitted to use campaign funds to pay for an expenditure that has a "political, legislative or governmental" purpose. Expenditures that confer a substantial personal benefit on the candidate or elected official must be directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose. (Section 89512(b). Section 89512(b), added in 2014, states that [e]xcept as expressly authorized by this article, an expenditure for a fine, penalty, judgment, or settlement is not within the lawful execution of the trust imposed by Section 89510."

Section 89513 addresses whether campaign funds may be used for specific activities. Under Section 89513(c), campaign funds may only be used to pay a fine, penalty, judgment, or settlement resulting from:

(1) Parking citations incurred in performance of an activity that was directly related to a political, legislative or governmental purpose.

(2) Any other action for which payment of attorneys' fees from contributions would be permitted under the Act. Section 89514 provides that expenditures of campaign funds for attorney's fees and other costs in connection with administrative, civil, or criminal litigation are only directly related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose where the litigation is directly related to activities of a committee that are consistent with its primary objectives or arises directly out of a committee's activities or out of a candidate's or elected officer's activities, duties, or status as a candidate or elected officer, including, but not limited to, an action to enjoin defamation, defense of an action brought for a violation of state or local campaign, disclosure, or election laws, and an action arising from an election contest or recount.

Section 89513(c) continues that in no event may campaign funds be used to pay or reimburse fines, penalties, judgments or settlements that resulted in:

(1) A personal benefit to the candidate or officer in violation of the personal use of campaign funds provisions; or

File No. A-16-035
Page No. 4

(2) A fine imposed for an offense of bribery under Section 86 of the Penal Code.

1. Where you have been named individually in an action as well as on behalf of two
committees, can you legally raise money in a "go-fund-me" style account from friends and
supporters to pay FPPC fines without involving the campaign committee?

No, under the Act you may not raise campaign funds to pay for fines in a "go-fund-me"
style account. Campaign funds for a state candidate must be raised into a committee and campaign
bank account under the recordkeeping and reporting rules and contribution limits of the Act.

2. If you raise money into the 2014 committee, which remains active because it has debt, can
those funds be used to pay FPPC fines related to your 2013 and 2014 committees, and if so, how is
that to be accounted for? Does it affect the debt total or is it a separate category?

Your 2014 committee is still active because you have $27,000 in campaign debt from the
special election. Given that paying the late filing fines is a permissible use of campaign funds under
Sections 89513(c) and 89514, and that the fines would constitute additional debt of the committee,
you may raise funds into the 2014 committee under the applicable contribution limit to pay those
fines.

A state candidate must establish a separate controlled committee and campaign bank
account for each election and expenses related to each election must come from the appropriate
campaign bank account. (Section 85201; Regulations 18521 and 18525.³) However, a candidate
may make payments from a current committee for fines and settlement costs incurred in connection
with reporting violations made by the candidate's committee from a prior election, if it is a
permissible use of funds under Section 89513 and 89514.

The rationale for this interpretation is as follows. If payment of the fine is permitted under
Sections 89513 and 89514, it meets the "political purpose" test for the use of campaign funds. And
although the Act's rules require committees to terminate in a timely manner after the election,⁴
fines, penalties, judgments or settlements, are often incurred by a candidate following an audit or
enforcement action that may conclude long after the election committee that made the reporting
violation has terminated. Rather than requiring a candidate at the end of an enforcement action to
re-open a terminated committee for the sole purpose of paying the fine, the candidate is permitted to
pay the fine from a current committee.

3. Can you open a legal defense committee to raise the funds? If you do, what are the
contribution limits and can donors who "maxed out" to your campaign still contribute to it?

Alternatively, you may open a legal defense fund to raise funds to pay for the fine for late
filing of campaign statements. As stated below, contribution limits do not apply to amounts raised

³ Regulation 18525 specifies that incumbent elected officers must make campaign-related expenses from the
appropriate campaign bank account for a future term of office, rather than from an officeholder account.

⁴ Section 84214; Regulations 18404 and 18404.1.

File No. A-16-035
Page No. 5

into a legal defense fund and donors who gave the maximum amount to your campaign committee
may contribute to it. (Section 85304 and Regulation 18530.4; Heneghan Advice Letter,
No. A-10-138.)

Section 85304 provides as follows concerning a state candidate's legal defense fund:

"(a) A candidate for elective state office or an elected state officer may
establish a separate account to defray attorney's fees and other related legal costs
incurred for the candidate's or officer's legal defense if the candidate or officer is
subject to one or more civil or criminal proceedings or administrative proceedings
arising directly out of the conduct of an election campaign, the electoral process, or
the performance of the officer's governmental activities and duties. These funds may
be used only to defray those attorney fees and other related legal costs.

(b) A candidate may receive contributions to this account that are not subject
to the contribution limits set forth in this article. However, all contributions shall be
reported in a manner prescribed by the commission.

(c) Once the legal dispute is resolved, the candidate shall dispose of any
funds remaining after all expenses associated with the dispute are discharged for one
or more of the purposes set forth in paragraphs (1) to (5), inclusive, of subdivision
(b) of Section 89519.

(d) (1) For purposes of this section and Section 85304.5, "attorney's fees and
other related legal costs" includes only the following:

(A) Attorney's fees and other legal costs related to the defense of the
candidate or officer.

(B) Administrative costs directly related to compliance with the requirements
of this title.

(2) "Attorney's fees and other related legal costs" does not include expenses
for fundraising, media or political consulting fees, mass mailing or other advertising,
or, except as expressly authorized by subdivision (c) of Section 89513,³ a payment or
reimbursement for a fine, penalty, judgment or settlement, or a payment to return or
disgorge contributions made to any other committee controlled by the candidate or
officer."

We have attached Regulation 18530.4, concerning legal defense funds for a state candidate.
To open a legal defense fund, you establish a separate controlled committee and bank account for
the legal defense fund by filing a statement of organization on Form 410. The statement of
organization must describe the specific legal dispute for which the account is established and the

³ Prior to 2014, Regulation 18530.4 stated that legal defense funds could not be used to pay fines, penalties,
judgments or settlements. Section 85304 was amended in 2014 allowing legal defense funds to be used to pay a fine,
penalty, judgment or settlement if permitted by Sections 89513(c) and 89514. Conforming amendments were made to
Regulation 18530.4(g)(1)(B) and 18530.4(i)(1)(B) in 2015.

File No. A-16-035
Page No. 6

words "Legal Defense Fund" and the candidate's last name must be included in the committee
name. Legal defense funds may only be raised in an amount reasonably calculated to pay attorney's
fees and other related legal costs.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel, Legal Division

HPW:jgl

Enclosure

fees and other costs in connection with administrative, civil, or criminal litigation are only directly 
related to a political, legislative, or governmental purpose where the litigation is directly related to 
activities of a committee that are consistent with its primary objectives or arises directly out of a 
committee's activities or out of a candidate's or elected officer's activities, duties, or status as a 
candidate or elected officer, including, but not limited to, an action to enjoin defamation, defense of 
an action to enjoin defamation, defense of an action brought for a violation of state or local 
campaign, disclosure, or election laws, and an action arising from an election contest or recount. 
Section 89513(c) continues that in no event may campaign funds be used to pay or 
reimburse fines, penalties, judgments or settlements that resulted in: 
(I) A personal benefit to the candidate or officer in violation of the personal use of 
campaign funds provisions; or  
(2) A fine imposed for an offense of bribery under Section 86 of the Penal Code. 
1. Where you have been named individually in an action as well as 011 be/zalf of two 
committees, can you legally raise money in a "go--fund-me "style account from friends and 
supporters to pay FPPCfines without involving t/ze campaign commitlee? 
No, under the Act you may not raise campaign funds to pay for fines in a "go-fund-me" 
style account. Campaign funds for a state candidate must be raised into a committee and campaign 
bank account under the recordkeeping and reporting rules and contribution limits of the Act. 
2. If you raise money into the 2014 committee, ·w/zic/z remains active because it /zas debt. can 
t/zose funds be used to pay FPPCfines related to your 2013 and 2014 committees. and if so. lzo1-1· is 
that to be accozmted for? Does it afect the debt total or is it a separate catego,y? 
Your 2014 committee is still active because you have $27,000 in campaign debt from the 
special election. Given that paying the late filing fines is a permissible use of campaign funds under 
Sections 89513( c) and 89514, and that the fines would constitute additional debt of the committee, 
you may raise funds into the 2014 committee under the applicable contribution limit to pay those 
fines. 
A state candidate must establish a separate controlled committee and campaign bank 
account for each election and expenses related to each election must come from the appropriate 
campaign bank account. (Section 85201; Regulations 18521 and 18525.3) However, a candidate 
may make payments from a current committee for fines and settlement costs incurred in connection 
with reporting violations made by the candidate's committee from a prior election, ifit is a 
permissible use of funds under Section 89513 and 89514. 
The rationale for this interpretation is as follows. If payment of the fine is permitted under 
Sections 89513 and 89514, it meets the "political purpose'' test for the use of campaign funds. And 
although the Act's rules require committees to terminate in a timely manner after the election,4 
fines, penalties, judgments or settlements, are often incurred by a candidate following an audit or 
enforcement action that may conclude long after the election committee that made the reporting 
violation has terminated. Rather than requiring a candidate at the end of an enforcement action to 
re-open a terminated committee for the sole purpose of paying the fine, the candidate is permitted to 
pay the fine from a current committee. 
3. Can you open a legal defense committee to raise thefimds? If you do. what are the 
contribution limits and can donors w/zo "maxed out" to your campaign still contribute to it? 
Alternatively, you may open a legal defense fund to raise funds to pay for the fine for late 
filing of campaign statements. As stated below, contribution limits do not apply to amounts raised 
3 Regulation 18525 specifies that incumbent elected officers must make campaign•related expenses from the 
appropriate campaign bank account for a future term of office, rather than from an officeholder account. 
4 Section 84214; Regulations 18404 and 18404.1.  
into a legal defense fund and donors who gave the maximum amount to your campaign committee 
may contribute to it. (Section 85304 and Regulation 18530.4; Hcmeghan Advice Letter, 
No. A-10-138.) 
Section 85304 provides as follows concerning a state candidate's legal defense fund: 
"(a) A candidate for elective state office or an elected state officer may 
establish a separate account to defray attorney's fees and other related legal costs 
incurred for the candidate's or officer's legal defense if the candidate or officer is 
subject to one or more civil or criminal proceedings or administrative proceedings 
arising directly out of the conduct of an election campaign, the electoral process, or 
the performance of the officer's governmental activities and duties. These funds may 
be used only to defray those attorney fees and other related legal costs. 
(b) A candidate may receive contributions to this account that are not subject 
to the contribution limits set forth in this article. However, all contributions shall be 
reported in a manner prescribed by the commission. 
( c) Once the legal dispute is resolved, the candidate shall dispose of any 
funds remaining after all expenses associated with the dispute are discharged for one 
or more of the purposes set forth in paragraphs ( 1) to (5), inclusive, of subdivision 
(b) of Section 89519. 
(d) (I) For purposes of this section and Section 85304.5, "attorney's fees and 
other related legal costs" includes only the following: 
(A) Attorney's fees and other legal costs related to the defense of the 
candidate or officer. 
(B) Administrative costs directly related to compliance with the requirements 
of this title. 
(2) "Attorney's fees and other related legal costs" does not include expenses 
for fundraising, media or political consulting fees, mass mailing or other advertising, 
or, except as expressly authorized by subdivision (c) of Section 89513,5 a payment or 
reimbursement for a fine, penalty, judgment or settlement, or a payment to return or 
disgorge contributions made to any other committee controlled by the candidate or 
officer." 
We have attached Regulation 18530.4, concerning legal defense funds for a state candidate. 
To open a legal defense fund, you establish a separate controlled committee and bank account for 
the legal defense fund by filing a statement of organization on Form 410. The statement of 
organization must describe the specific legal dispute for which the account is established and the 
5 Prior to 2014, Regulation 18530.4 stated that legal defense funds could not be used to pay fines, penalties, 
judgments or settlements. Section 85304 was amended in 20 I 4 allowing legal defense funds to be used to pay a fine, 
penalty,judgment or settlement ifpennitted by Sections 89513(c) and 89514. Conforming amendments were made to 
Regulation 18530.4{g)(l)(B) and 18530.45(i){l)(B) in 2015.  
words "Legal Defense Fund" and the candidate's last name must be included in the committee 
name. Legal defense funds may only be raised in an amount reasonably calculated to pay attorney's 
fees and other related legal costs.
embedding.first_500_words: STATE OF CALIFORNIA FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 March 10, 2016 Ms. Susan Shelley P.O. Box 6823 Woodland Hills, CA 91365 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-16-035 Dear Ms. Shelley: This letter responds to your request for advice from the Fair Political Practices Commission ("FPPC") regarding campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ QUESTIONS 1. Where you have been named individually in an enforcement action as well as on behalf of two committees for filing campaign statements late, can you legally raise money in a "go-fund-me" style account from friends and supporters to pay FPPC fines without involving the campaign committee? 2. If you raise money into the "Susan Shelley for Assembly 2014" committee, ID #1355796 (the "2014 committee"), which remains active because it has debt, can those funds be used to pay FPPC fines, and if so, how is that to be accounted for? Does it affect the debt total or is it a separate category? 3. Can you open a legal defense committee to raise the funds? If you do, what are the contribution limits and can donors who "maxed out" to your campaign still contribute to it? CONCLUSIONS 1. No, under the Act you may not raise campaign funds to pay for fines in a "go-fund-me" style account. 2. Given that paying the late filing fines is a permissible use of campaign funds under Sections 89513(c) and 89514, and that the fines would constitute additional debt of the committee, you may raise funds into the 2014 committee under the applicable contribution limit to pay those fines. ¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-16-035 Page No. 2 3. Yes. Under the Act you are also permitted to open a legal defense fund to raise funds to pay the fine for late filing of campaign statements. As discussed below, contribution limits do not apply to amounts raised into a legal defense fund, and donors who gave the maximum amount to your campaign committee may contribute to the legal defense fund. (Section 85304 and Regulation 18530.4.) FACTS² You were a candidate for Assembly District 45 in the 2013 special primary and general elections, and ran again for that seat in the June 3, 2014 primary election. You did not win either election. You have been informed that the FPPC is pursuing an enforcement action against you for campaign finance reporting errors and omissions from your 2013 special election campaign for Assembly District 45. Because of the circumstances of the special election – the incumbent Assembly member had
